detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the height (Â± 0.5 cm) of a professional golf player who has participated in the 2022-23 season of their respective golf tours (PGA Tour, DP World Tour, and LIV Golf ) affect their average driving distance? (Â± 1.5 yards)

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: Clear personal significance established through family background and golfing aspirations
Independent selection of height bands, statistical tests, and qualitative observations
Sustained initiative evident in bespoke data compilation and narrative positioning
weaknesses: Duplicate entry for participant 39 in processed data table introduces potential bias
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates clear and sustained personal engagement through independent question design, data choices, and narrative framing, meriting the top band (2/2). The only notable issue is a duplicated data entry, which does not detract from the overall strong personal input and initiative.

strengths: Research question is relevant and focused on height versus driving distance
Background integrates biomechanical theory and tour statistics
Methodology outlines variables, statistical tests, and confounders
Ethical considerations address anonymity and minimal safety concerns
weaknesses: Question wording is verbose and could be more concise
Several biomechanical claims lack proper academic citations
Data-usage permissions and exclusion criteria for incomplete records are not specified
Ethics discussion omits issues of data-source permissions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration shows a relevant, mostly focused question, appropriate background and methodology, and some ethical awareness, placing it solidly in the mid band (3/6). However, verbosity, citation gaps, and methodological omissions prevent a higher score.

strengths: Comprehensive raw data from three tours with relevant variables
Consistent use of formulas for means, SDs, CVs, Pearson r and t-tests
Inclusion of absolute uncertainties and error bars for group means
Broadly valid interpretation linking statistical outputs to conclusions
weaknesses: Data set contains duplicates and uneven sampling (e.g., limited LIV data)
Rounding inconsistencies and a mismatch between plotted trendline and reported r value
Uncertainty propagation and individual-value errors are not calculated or discussed
Figures lack descriptive captions and detailed in-text discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates relevant raw data, appropriate processing, uncertainty consideration, and valid interpretation, warranting the mid band (3/6). Errors in data integrity, rounding, and incomplete uncertainty treatment limit it from a higher band.

strengths: Conclusion cites mean values and p-values to support findings
Comparison to external studies shows some scientific context
Suggestions for improvement are realistic and relevant
weaknesses: No confidence intervals or effect sizes are incorporated into the conclusion
Context comparisons are brief and lack full justification
Outlier handling criteria and its impact are not fully explained
Improvement suggestions lack detailed experimental design steps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation provides a relevant, supported conclusion, context comparison, limitations discussion, and improvement ideas, placing it in the mid band (3/6). A deeper integration of statistical metrics and richer detail in comparisons and procedures is needed for higher marks.

strengths: Well-structured report following IB conventions with clear sectioning
Appropriate and consistent use of SEHS terminology and units
Presentation is clear and concise, facilitating reader understanding
Content is focused and relevant, with minimal clutter
weaknesses: Occasional typographical errors and inconsistent table caption formatting
Axes labels on graphs lack specific unit notation and formatting consistency
Citation style is mixed; some entries lack complete publication details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication is clear, well organized, and uses terminology correctly, aligning with the top band (4/4). Minor formatting and citation inconsistencies prevent an absolutely flawless presentation but do not significantly impede understanding.

strandSelections.reasoning: Absolute uncertainties for height and distance are stated and error bars for grouped means are provided, indicating consideration, but there is no propagation, no discussion of percentage error, and individual-value uncertainties are absent.

Several external studies (Blazevich, Tutelman) are discussed and compared to the studentâs results, demonstrating some engagement with scientific context, yet the evaluation of agreement/discrepancy is brief and not fully justified.

Sections follow the IB structure (RQ, method, data, evaluation); all essential information is present, though the appendix is unwieldy and occasional table repetition affects smooth flow, hence solid but not exemplary organisation.

For each limitation the student proposes improvements such as primary data collection and controlled environments; suggestions are generally realistic and relevant but not elaborated into detailed, feasible experimental designs.

The research question is relevant and mostly focusedâheight (Â±0.5 cm) versus average driving distance (Â±1.5 yd) in 2022-23 professional toursâbut the wording is verbose and includes three tours in one sentence, slightly diluting precision.

A table outlines multiple limitations (course conditions, replicability, bias) with explanation, evidencing methodological awareness.  Still, some entries lack depth or specific quantitative influence, so it sits in the mid-band.

Using secondary data is sensible for this topic; the method identifies variables, height bands, statistical tests and confounders, but does not detail sample sizes per band or criteria for excluding incomplete cases.  Thus it is mainly appropriate yet with clear limitations.

Background information explains biomechanical theories, modern equipment, cited studies (Tutelman) and tour averages; although relevant and largely appropriate, some paraphrasing is superficial and several claims lack scholarly referencing, so it falls short of âentirely appropriate and comprehensiveâ.

Most content is relevant to the investigation; while some long tables and anecdotal observations add minor clutter, they do not seriously obscure understanding, maintaining acceptable focus and concision.

Interpretation references statistical outputs, notes weak correlation, and links to figures, yielding broadly valid conclusions.  However, some statements (e.g., âno correlationâ despite significant short-vs-medium t-test) show partial inconsistency, preventing a fully detailed, correct explanation.

The report is logically sectioned with numbered tables and figures; despite some formatting breaks and duplicated IDs, the narrative can be followed with minimal difficulty, meeting the clear-presentation requirement.

The student places himself centrally in the narrative, explaining his long-standing interest in golf, the personal relevance of height to his own game and his decision to study professional statistics.  He chooses the variables, designs the height-band categories and selects statistical tests without relying on teacher templates, showing independent thinking and sustained initiative.

Processing includes means, standard deviations, CVs, Pearson r and t-tests; formulae are shown and values calculated consistently.  Nonetheless, rounding inconsistencies (e.g., CV example) and an r that does not match the plotted trendline reveal moderate inaccuracy.

The opening background section explicitly connects the investigation to the studentâs family, varsity aspirations and perceived disadvantage as a shorter golfer.  This establishes clear personal significance and genuine curiosity about whether height actually matters in professional play.

An extensive appendix of heights and distances from three tours is provided, showing relevance, but gaps (e.g., duplicated IDs, limited LIV sample) mean the set is not entirely complete or evenly distributed.

Personal input is visible in the bespoke grouping of heights, compilation of three professional-tour databases, choice of t-tests/pearson r, and construction of limitation tables.  The studentâs own qualitative video observations further evidence initiative across design, implementation and presentation.

The student recognises ethical anonymity and argues that safety is negligible, demonstrating some awareness.  However, the discussion is brief and does not address data-usage permissions or broader ethical issues around professional statistics, so it remains partial rather than âfullâ awareness.

Key SEHS terms (standard deviation, coefficient of variation, torque) are used correctly and units are generally consistent; a few typographical errors ("Oualitative") and inconsistent symbol formatting prevent a âminimal-errorâ judgement of 4, but proficiency is evident.
The conclusion restates main findings and cites mean values and P-values, giving reasonable support, but does not integrate confidence intervals or effect sizes, so depth is intermediate.

annotations.comment: You cite Trackman uncertainty but should describe how Â±1.5 yd and Â±0.5 cm affect group means and confidence intervals in the bar chart.
Qualitative swing observations are interesting but lack systematic criteria. Consider defining tempo metrics and using software to quantify backswing/downswing times.
Processed data table contains duplicate entries for participant 39, which can bias statistical measures. Remove or mark duplicates to maintain data integrity.
Table 3 caption formatting is inconsistent with other tables and interrupts flow; standardize caption placement and numbering for clarity.
Reporting the short vs medium p-value as â0.00â is misleading; either show more decimal places or state p < 0.001 to accurately reflect significance.
Maintain consistent decimal places across all statistical tables. Aligning decimals improves readability and prevents confusion.
Axes on both graphs lack specific unit labels and consistent formatting. Include units and uncertainty notation directly on axes for clear interpretation.
Figures lack descriptive captions and in-text discussion. Add concise captions and refer to key trends in the narrative to guide reader interpretation.
The research question is verbose, combining three tours in one sentence and diluting focus. Refine it to specify the height and driving distance variables clearly and concisely.
Several biomechanical claims in the background lack proper citation. Integrate peer-reviewed studies for statements on centrifugal force and launch angle to strengthen the literature base.
The hypothesis is stated generally but would benefit from quantifying the expected difference (e.g., yards per cm of height) to guide your statistical testing and interpretation.
Controlled variables in Table 1 are listed but the description is cut off. Clarify each control (e.g., minimum tournaments played) with explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria.
Ethical considerations address anonymity but omit data usage permissions from tour websites. Discuss whether secondary data usage complies with copyright or privacy policies.
Uncertainty table is empty; include calculated uncertainties and discuss how they propagate through mean and t-test computations to demonstrate proper error analysis.
The methodology list outlines data collection but omits rules for excluding incomplete records. Specify how missing height or drive values will be handled.
Discussion of torque and lever arm from Blazevich and Tutelman is robust, but linking it directly to your data would strengthen the scientific context comparison.
Sample calculation table lists formulas but lacks a worked example of Pearsonâs r and t-tests with numerical inputs. Include detailed examples for transparency.
Inference section cites one outlier but does not explain removal criteria or its statistical impact. Discuss how the outlier affects mean and correlation.
The concluding paragraph restates averages and p-values but omits confidence intervals or effect sizes. Incorporate these metrics to fully support your conclusion.
Evaluation of strengths mentions data accuracy but should reference specific measurement methods (e.g., laser Trackman precision) to substantiate claims of minimal error.
Limitations table lists improvements but lacks procedural detail for primary data collection. Outline experimental design steps to show feasibility.
Works Cited entries use mixed formats (quotations, missing years). Adopt a consistent citation style and include complete publication details in each entry.
